children.name: Aptenodytes
children.description: The great penguins — the largest and most iconic penguin species, comprising the Emperor and King Penguins. Aptenodytes species are distinguished by their large size, vivid orange-yellow ear patches, and a unique breeding biology: Kings incubate eggs on their feet over winter, while Emperors breed on Antarctic sea ice in the dead of winter, enduring temperatures below -60°C.

children.name: Aptenodytes forsteri
children.description: The tallest and heaviest of all penguins, reaching 122 cm and 45 kg. Endemic to Antarctica. The only vertebrate to breed during the Antarctic winter, with males incubating the single egg on their feet for 65 days in total darkness, enduring winds of 200 km/h and temperatures of -60°C. Females return from feeding at sea just as the chick hatches. Featured in the film March of the Penguins.

children.name: Aptenodytes patagonicus
children.description: The second-largest penguin, reaching 95 cm and 18 kg. Breeds on subantarctic islands including South Georgia, Crozet, and the Falklands. Its orange ear patches are the most vivid of any penguin. The breeding cycle takes 14-16 months, among the longest of any bird. Colonies can number hundreds of thousands of individuals.

children.name: Pygoscelis
children.description: Brush-tailed penguins — medium-sized Antarctic penguins named for their long, stiff tail feathers that brush the ground when walking. They are the most abundant penguins on Earth, with Adélie and Chinstrap colonies numbering in the millions. All three species breed on the Antarctic continent and subantarctic islands.

children.name: Pygoscelis adeliae
children.description: One of the most abundant penguins, with an estimated 10 million individuals. Identified by the all-dark head with a white eye-ring. Nests on the Antarctic coast and surrounding islands, building pebble nests on ice-free ground. Named after Adélie Land, the French Antarctic territory named by explorer Jules Dumont d'Urville after his wife.

children.name: Pygoscelis antarcticus
children.description: Instantly recognisable by the narrow black band under the chin that gives the species its name. Breeds on Antarctic and subantarctic islands in huge colonies. The most aggressive of the brush-tailed penguins, often nesting on steep slopes. Roughly 8 million pairs worldwide.

children.name: Pygoscelis papua
children.description: The largest brush-tailed penguin, weighing up to 8.5 kg. Distinguished by a white patch above each eye and a bright orange-red bill. The fastest swimming penguin, reaching 36 km/h. Breeds in colonies on Antarctic and subantarctic islands. Gentoo populations in the western Antarctic Peninsula are among the fastest-warming regions on Earth.

children.name: Eudyptes
children.description: The crested penguins — medium-sized penguins with prominent yellow or orange feather crests above the eyes. Found on subantarctic and temperate islands of the Southern Ocean. Most species have a distinctive 'bouncing' walk and a harsh, braying call. Several species are among the most threatened penguins, with populations declining sharply due to climate change and overfishing.

children.name: Eudyptes chrysolophus
children.description: The most numerous crested penguin, with around 18 million individuals. Black head, chin, and throat with a spectacular golden-yellow crest that meets across the forehead. Named after the 'macaroni' fashion of 18th-century English dandies. Breeds in dense colonies on subantarctic islands.

children.name: Eudyptes chrysocome
children.description: The smallest crested penguin, known for its striking yellow-and-black head plumes, red eyes, and pink feet. Named for its habit of hopping over rocks rather than sliding on its belly. Breeds on subantarctic islands around the tip of South America. Populations have declined by 30% in the last three decades.

children.name: Eudyptes schlegeli
children.description: Endemic to Macquarie Island, where nearly the entire population breeds. Very similar to the Macaroni Penguin but with a white face and chin. Fluctuating populations make it one of the less studied crested penguins.

children.name: Eudyptes filholi
children.description: Breeds on subantarctic islands in the Indian and Pacific Oceans. Recently split from the Southern Rockhopper. Distinguished by a narrower supercilium and slightly larger size. Nests on rocky cliffs and slopes.

children.name: Eudyptes pachyrhynchus
children.description: A crested penguin endemic to New Zealand's Fiordland region. Dark blue-grey upperparts with a thick yellow crest. The least studied of all penguin species, nesting in dense temperate rainforest under giant ferns and tree roots. Classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Eudyptes sclateri
children.description: A crested penguin endemic to the Bounty and Antipodes Islands of New Zealand, with a distinctive upright yellow crest that meets at the top of the head. It is one of the least studied penguins and is classified as Endangered. Its population has declined significantly due to climate change and changes in prey availability.

children.name: Megadyptes
children.description: The yellow-eyed penguin — a monotypic genus endemic to New Zealand. Distinguished by its pale yellow eyes and yellow head band. Considered the rarest penguin species, with around 4,000 individuals remaining. It is the only penguin with a solitary, non-colonial breeding style, nesting in coastal scrub and forest.

children.name: Megadyptes antipodes
children.description: The world's rarest penguin, endemic to the southeastern coast of New Zealand and its subantarctic islands. Distinctive yellow eyes and a yellow band wrapping around the head. Unlike other penguins, it nests in scattered pairs in coastal forest and scrub, not in dense colonies. Classified as Endangered. Known in Māori as Hoiho.

children.name: Eudyptula
children.description: The little penguins — the smallest penguins in the world, found only in Australia and New Zealand. Nocturnal on land to avoid predators, they return to their burrows after dark in 'penguin parades' that have become major tourist attractions. Their slate-blue plumage gives them the alternative name 'fairy penguins'.

children.name: Eudyptula minor
children.description: The smallest penguin, standing only 33 cm tall and weighing 1.5 kg. Found along the coasts of southern Australia, Tasmania, and New Zealand. Slate-blue upperparts, white underparts. Nocturnal on land, returning ashore after dark in groups. The penguin parade at Phillip Island in Victoria is one of Australia's most famous wildlife spectacles.

children.name: Eudyptula albosignata
children.description: A small penguin endemic to the Banks Peninsula and Motunau Island of New Zealand's South Island. It is distinguished from the Little Penguin by a broader white trailing edge on the flipper. It nests in burrows and under vegetation along the rocky coastline. Its taxonomic status is debated, with some authorities treating it as a subspecies of Eudyptula minor.

children.name: Spheniscus
children.description: The banded penguins — medium-sized penguins of temperate and tropical coasts, distinguished by a characteristic black band across the chest. Unlike Antarctic penguins, they inhabit warmer waters and have bare facial patches that help dissipate heat. The genus includes the only penguin species found north of the Equator (Galápagos Penguin).

children.name: Spheniscus demersus
children.description: The only penguin breeding in Africa, found along the coast of Namibia and South Africa. Distinctive black band and horseshoe-shaped black spots. Colonies have declined by 95% since the 19th century due to egg harvesting, guano mining, and overfishing. Now classified as Endangered. Known locally as the Jackass Penguin for its donkey-like bray.

children.name: Spheniscus magellanicus
children.description: The most abundant banded penguin, breeding on the coasts of Argentina, Chile, and the Falkland Islands. Two black bands across the chest. Named after Ferdinand Magellan, whose expedition first recorded the species in 1520. The largest breeding colony is at Punta Tombo, Argentina, with over 500,000 pairs.

children.name: Spheniscus humboldti
children.description: Breeding on the coasts of Peru and Chile, influenced by the cold Humboldt Current. One black band across the chest. Smaller than the Magellanic Penguin with a distinctive fleshy pink base to the bill. Classified as Vulnerable due to El Niño events, overfishing, and guano harvesting.

children.name: Spheniscus mendiculus
children.description: The only penguin found north of the Equator, endemic to the Galápagos Islands. The rarest banded penguin, with fewer than 2,000 individuals. Survives in the tropics due to the cold Cromwell Current bringing nutrient-rich waters. Highly vulnerable to El Niño events.
